image.alt: Boardroom table with notebooks and coffee cups in warm afternoon light

situation.headline: The situation
paragraphs: A software founder closed a $48M acquisition. Ninety percent of the proceeds sat in a single stock position with the acquiring company. The lock-up period was 18 months.

The founder had no prior relationship with a wealth manager. Tax exposure from the sale was significant, and the concentrated holding carried meaningful downside risk. The founder needed a clear plan before the lock-up expired.

approach.headline: Our approach
paragraphs: We started with a full financial picture: existing assets, tax obligations, living expenses, and long-term goals. From that, we built a staged liquidation schedule aligned to the lock-up timeline.

We used tax-loss harvesting across the broader portfolio to offset gains from each tranche sale. Asset location decisions placed tax-inefficient holdings inside retirement accounts and tax-efficient positions in taxable accounts. Every trade was documented in plain language.

outcome.headline: The outcome
paragraphs: Over the 18-month liquidation window, the concentrated position dropped from 90% to under 15% of total holdings. The diversified portfolio now spans domestic equities, international developed markets, fixed income, and alternatives.

Tax-loss harvesting saved the client six figures in the first year. The client receives quarterly reports and has a written investment policy that governs every future decision. The relationship is now in its fourth year.
